questions.question: On ASME Code work, the shop wants to change the procedure's excess-penetrant removal from lipophilic emulsifier to hydrophilic remover. Under Section V, Article 6, how is that change handled?
options: Note the change in the inspector's daily log, since both are post-emulsifiable methods
Removal method is an essential variable — the procedure must be revised and, where demonstration is specified, requalified before use
Treat it as a nonessential variable, like post-examination cleaning technique, requiring revision only
No action is needed as long as both removers appear on the qualified products list
questions.explanation: ASME Section V, Article 6 lists the removal method among a procedure's essential variables: change one and the procedure must be revised and, where demonstration is specified, requalified. Nonessential variables such as post-examination cleaning technique need revision only — and a QPL listing qualifies the material, not the procedure that uses it.

questions.question: During the completeness pass on a draft written to ASTM E1417, which requirement passes the arithmetic audit against the practice's limits?
options: Minimum penetrant dwell of 5 minutes
Hydrophilic remover sprayed at 10 % concentration
Drying with part surface temperature held at 150 °F
Fluorescent examination with ambient white light at 8 fc
questions.explanation: E1417 sets minimum dwell at 10 minutes, caps hydrophilic spray concentration at 5 % (35 % for immersion), limits part surface temperature during drying to 160 °F, and holds ambient white light in the fluorescent booth to 2 fc or less. Only the 150 °F dryer sits inside its limit — each other option violates one number.

questions.question: Wednesday morning's system-performance check on the known-defect standard fails. Which parts must be reexamined?
options: Every part processed since the last successful system-performance check, as bounded by the dated process-control log
Only the part that was on the line when the check failed
All parts processed in the previous calendar quarter, regardless of the logs
None, provided the radiometer is still within its calibration interval
questions.explanation: A failed daily check puts every part processed since the last successful check in question, and those parts are reexamined. The signed, dated process-control log is what bounds that recall to one shift instead of one quarter — instrument calibration proves the meter, not that the penetrant process was in control.

questions.question: A part is rejected at your bench. Beyond completing the examination report, what does the documentation system require for the hardware itself?
options: Scrap it immediately so it cannot reenter production
Return it to production with a verbal warning to the area supervisor
Positively identify and segregate it, tied to a nonconformance report number so the trail continues past your bench
Mark it accepted-pending-review so it can keep moving while engineering decides
questions.explanation: Rejected parts are positively identified, segregated, and linked to an NCR number — the paper trail must continue into the quality system. Final disposition (scrap, rework, use-as-is) belongs to the material review process, not the inspector, and no part moves under an acceptance status it has not earned.

questions.question: A job package invokes ASTM E1417 and your written procedure, but no acceptance criteria were flowed down. A crisp relevant indication is on the part. What is the correct action?
options: Evaluate it against ASME Appendix 8, since that pattern is the industry default
Accept the part if the indication looks minor for the application
Apply the indication size limits published in E1417 itself
Stop — you cannot disposition the part; obtain written direction from the cognizant engineering organization
questions.explanation: Method practices control how to test, never whether the part is acceptable — E1417 has no size-limit annex, and borrowing another code's table because it looks similar means evaluating to a requirement nobody authorized. With no flowed-down criteria, processing stops until the cognizant engineering organization provides written direction.

questions.question: On a vessel weld evaluated to the typical ASME Section VIII, Division 1, Appendix 8 pattern, you find four relevant rounded indications, each 1/8 in. across, in a line spaced 1/16 in. edge to edge. What is the disposition?
options: Accept — each indication is under the 3/16 in. rounded limit
Reject — rounded indications are never permitted on pressure-vessel welds
Not relevant — rounded indications under 3/16 in. fall below the relevance threshold
Reject — four or more rounded indications in a line spaced 1/16 in. or less edge to edge fail the alignment rule, even though each alone would pass
questions.explanation: Individually, each 1/8 in. rounded indication is inside the 3/16 in. limit — but the Appendix 8 pattern also rejects four or more rounded indications in a line when edge-to-edge spacing is 1/16 in. or less. The relevance threshold is a 1/16 in. major dimension, which these far exceed; alignment is a separate, additional test.

questions.question: Nozzle weld N-2 was rejected for a relevant linear indication, weld-repaired, and returned to your bench still carrying patches of the original developer. Production asks you to redevelop the repaired area and read it. What does the inspection cycle require?
options: Full reprocessing from Step 0 — clean, penetrant, removal, developer — and evaluation to the same acceptance criteria
Redevelop only, since penetrant from the first test is still in any remaining flaws
A visual inspection of the repair is sufficient after a documented PT rejection
Reexamine only if the repair crossed the original indication's recorded location
questions.explanation: A repaired area reenters the cycle at Step 0 and is completely reprocessed — precleaning through development — then evaluated to the same flowed-down criteria. Residue from the failed examination proves nothing about the new surface; a fresh coat of developer over the old process is not a re-examination.

questions.question: In the six-section skeleton of a written PT procedure, where does the order of precedence among conflicting documents belong?
options: In the reporting section, since conflicts surface when reports are reviewed
In the foreword, alongside the scope statement and the reference documents
In the detailed instructions, where the inspector will actually read it
Nowhere — precedence is decided case by case by the lead inspector
questions.explanation: The foreword is the gatekeeper: scope, reference documents with their revisions, and the order of precedence that settles conflicts before work starts — typically purchase order or contract first, then customer specification, then the procedure and the practice beneath it. Precedence is written down in advance, never improvised at the bench.

questions.question: Where does the real fire risk live on a typical penetrant line, and why?
options: The open penetrant dip tank, because of its large exposed liquid surface
The water-wash station, where rinse mist can carry penetrant onto ignition sources
The aerosol solvent removers and nonaqueous developer carriers — commonly Class I materials whose vapors can ignite at room temperature
The dry-developer chamber, because suspended powder detonates like grain dust
questions.explanation: AMS 2644 requires qualified penetrants to flash no lower than 93 °C (200 °F), so the open dip tank gives off no ignitable vapor at shop temperature. The Class I solvents in aerosol removers and nonaqueous developers flash below 100 °F — which is why only the working supply stays at the station, the rest lives in a flammable-liquids cabinet, and nothing gets sprayed near hot surfaces.

questions.question: Why are UV-absorbing spectacles specified for inspectors working at a fluorescent penetrant booth?
options: They block UV-A that would otherwise enter the eye and make its own lens fluoresce into a veiling haze — protecting the inspector and making indications easier to see
They darken automatically under booth UV to shield the retina
They amplify the yellow-green indication light the way night-vision optics do
They are required only when the lamp's filter is cracked or missing
questions.explanation: A working beam delivers at least 1,000 µW/cm² — the same order as the industrial-hygiene guideline for prolonged near-UV exposure of the unprotected eye — and UV-A entering the eye makes the lens itself fluoresce, casting a haze over the dark field. UV-absorbing spectacles close both problems at once; a cracked filter, by contrast, takes the lamp out of service — no eyewear compensates for it.
